2026-05-12.title: Colordle #1509 Answer: Cloud (#E4F0EF) – May 12, 2026
2026-05-12.summary: Today's answer is Cloud, a pale cyan-white that sits right on the edge of visibility. After yesterday's deep Sangria, this light-neutral feels like a palate cleanser. The hex #E4F0EF tricks players who expect more saturation. Use high-value greens and cyans to lock in the distance before guessing the exact tint.
bonusHints: Think of the color of skim milk with a drop of teal.
The green channel is slightly higher than red or blue.
It is much lighter than standard mint or seafoam.
Avoid guessing pure grays; there is definite chroma here.
2026-05-12.articleHtml:
Today's Colordle answer at a glance

Cloud (#E4F0EF) hit on May 12, 2026, as puzzle #1509, and it is a classic example of why you should never ignore the lightness metric. This is a light-neutral color, sitting firmly in the pale cyan family. If your first guess was a standard white or a light gray, you probably felt the distance meter spike because you missed the subtle cool undertone.

The hex code breaks down to high values across the board: R228, G240, B239. Notice how the green channel peaks at 240, pushing it away from a neutral gray and into that very faint minty territory. It is the kind of color that looks like white on a bad monitor but reveals its true nature on a calibrated screen. After the heavy, saturated Sangria yesterday, this feels almost like a trick question, but the logic holds if you trust the distance numbers.

What kind of color this is

Cloud lives in that tricky space between off-white and pastel. It is not quite gray, not quite blue, and definitely not green enough to be called mint. In design terms, we call this a high-value, low-chroma cyan. It is the color of a overcast sky just before it starts raining, or the foam on a cappuccino that has sat for too long.

The challenge with colors like this is that human eyes are terrible at distinguishing slight tints in high-lightness ranges. We are great at telling dark red from dark brown, but put two near-white colors side by side, and most people just see "light." That is why Cloud feels so slippery. It lacks the aggressive saturation of Mustard or the deep warmth of Mocha from earlier this week. It demands precision in your green and blue channels, not just your overall brightness.

How to narrow your guesses toward it

If you are stuck on a color like this, stop guessing random pastels. You need to isolate the hue first. Start with a pure, bright cyan like #00FFFF or a strong mint like #98FF98. Watch how the hue shift indicator moves. If the game tells you to go cooler, you are likely aiming for the blue side of cyan. If it says warmer, you might be drifting into pure green territory.

Once you have the hue locked, attack the lightness. Cloud is exceptionally bright. If your guess is #C0E0DF, you are already too dark. You need to push your RGB values well above 200. A good strategy here is to guess a color like #E0F0F0. It is close enough to act as a anchor. If the distance is still high, check your saturation. Cloud is desaturated. If your guess is a vivid electric blue, dial the saturation down until it looks almost boring. The answer usually hides in the "boring" zone for these light neutrals.

Where players usually go wrong

The biggest trap with Cloud is assuming it is a neutral gray. Players see #E4F0EF and think "light gray," guessing something like #E0E0E0. The game penalizes this heavily because the hue shift is significant. Even though the color looks grayish, that extra bit of green and blue changes everything. Treating it as a monochrome problem will leave you spinning your wheels for ten minutes.

Another common mistake is over-saturating. When players realize it is not gray, they swing the pendulum too far and guess a bright sky blue or a vivid seafoam. Cloud is subtle. It is a whisper of color, not a shout. If your guess looks like a crayon color, you have gone too far. The sweet spot is where the color almost disappears against a white background. That is where you will find the answer.

How the score logic actually helps

Colordle's distance algorithm is your best friend here because it quantizes what your eyes can't easily see. When you guess a color like #FFFFFF (pure white) and the distance is, say, 15, but your guess of #E4F0EF yields a distance of 2, the math is telling you that the chroma is the differentiator. The game calculates the Euclidean distance in 3D space. A small shift in hue at high brightness creates a larger distance than you might expect visually.

Use the hue shift arrows. They are more reliable than the distance number for these pale colors. If the arrow points slightly toward green, trust it, even if the color looks blueish to you. The algorithm does not care about your monitor's white balance; it cares about the raw hex values. If you align your guess to the hue arrow and match the lightness of the target, the distance will collapse rapidly. Do not ignore the directional hints just because the color looks ambiguous.

Questions players keep asking

Is Cloud considered a warm or cool color?

It is definitively cool. The blue and green components dominate the red channel. While it is close to neutral, the undertone is icy. If you place it next to a warm beige, the contrast will make it look distinctly blue-green. In color theory, anything with more blue/green than red/yellow leans cool, and Cloud fits this perfectly.

Why does it look different on my phone?

High-lightness, low-chroma colors are the first casualty of poor screen calibration. Phones often boost saturation and contrast to make images pop, which can make Cloud look like a distinct mint green on one device and a flat gray on another. This is why relying on the numerical feedback in the game is crucial when the visual feedback is inconsistent.

How is this different from the Lavender from two days ago?

Lavender (#B56EDC) is a mid-tone with significant saturation and a purple hue. It has presence. Cloud (#E4F0EF) is a high-tone with minimal saturation. Lavender pulls your eye immediately; Cloud recedes. Structurally, Lavender has a strong red and blue mix with low green, while Cloud has balanced high values with a green bias. They occupy opposite ends of the saturation spectrum.

What is the best first guess for colors like this?

Start with a range of lightness. Guess a pure white, a mid-gray, and a light pastel cyan. This triangulates the target quickly. If white is closer than gray, you know you are in the high-value zone. If cyan is closer than gray, you know there is chroma involved. Three guesses can usually isolate a color like Cloud to a tiny window of possibility.

2026-05-11.title: Colordle #1508: Sangria (#B14566) Answer & Hints
2026-05-11.summary: Today's answer is Sangria, a deep red-wine pink with hex #B14566. It sits firmly in the red family but leans heavily into magenta territory. If you wasted guesses on orange-reds or bright crimsons, the score likely punished you. Here is how to lock down this specific shade next time.
bonusHints: Think less 'fire engine' and more 'dried rose petal'.
The blue component is significant enough to kill pure red guesses.
It is darker than you probably guessed on your first try.
Avoid the orange side of the spectrum entirely.
2026-05-11.articleHtml:
Today's Colordle answer at a glance

Sangria is the answer for May 11, 2026. The hex code is #B14566. If you threw out a guess like #FF0000 or #DC143C (Crimson), you probably saw the game tell you that you were close on hue but way off on saturation or temperature. This isn't a primary red. It is a muted, wine-dark pink that lives in that uncomfortable valley between red and purple where a lot of players panic.

The name gives it away if you know your drinks. Sangria isn't bright cherry juice; it is stewed fruit and red wine. The color reflects that. It has a heaviness to it. In the recent history, we have swung from the electric yellow of Lemon two days ago to the earthy neutrality of Mocha, and now we are back in deep, saturated territory. But do not let the red family label fool you into thinking this is straightforward. It is not.

What kind of color this is

Sangria is a desaturated dark pink-red. Technically, it is a shade of rose or raspberry, but "Sangria" captures the mood better. Look at the hex breakdown: B1 for red, 45 for green, and 66 for blue. That red channel is dominant, sure, but look at the blue. At 66 (hex), the blue presence is strong enough to pull this away from orange-reds and firmly into magenta territory.

Compare this to yesterday's Lavender. Lavender was light and airy; Sangria is dense. Compare it to Mocha from two days ago. Mocha had brown in it; Sangria has purple in it. The difference is subtle but critical. If your guess came back looking like a tomato, you were too warm. If it looked like a Barbie doll, you were too light and too saturated. This color lives in the mid-tones with a heavy hand on the desaturation slider. It is the color of a bruised apple or a sunset that is about to turn into night.

How to narrow your guesses toward it

When you see the feedback indicating "Red" but your pure red guess feels wrong, stop guessing random reds. You need to test the temperature. Throw out a pure magenta or a pure purple immediately. If the game tells you that you are getting colder on hue but closer on value, you know you are dealing with a red-purple mix. Then, test the brightness. Guess a very dark version of that mix, and then a very light one.

Most players fail here because they assume "Red" means "Fire." They guess #FF4500 (OrangeRed) or #B22222 (FireBrick). Both are wrong for Sangria. FireBrick is too brown/orange. You need to introduce blue to your red. Start with a standard pink like #FFC0CB. If the game says you are too light, darken it. If it says you are too saturated, mute it by adding gray or moving the RGB values closer together. The key to Sangria is that middle ground where red stops being aggressive and starts being moody.

Where players usually go wrong

The biggest trap today is the name association. People hear "Sangria" and think of the bright red fruit chunks floating in a pitcher. They guess a vibrant, high-saturation red. But the drink itself, the liquid, is often darker and muddier than the fruit. Players also struggle with the blue component. In the RGB model, adding blue to red makes purple. But if you only add a little bit, you get these wine colors. Players often overshoot and go full purple, or undershoot and stay in bright pink land.

Another common mistake is ignoring the green channel. In #B14566, the green value is 45. That is not zero. A pure red-purple would have lower green. That bit of green is what kills the vibrancy and gives it that dusty, organic feel. If your guess looks like it belongs on a neon sign, you have neglected the green channel entirely. You need that slight muddiness to hit the target. Do not be afraid to guess colors that look a bit "dirty" or grayish. Clean colors rarely hit the mark in the mid-to-dark ranges.

How the score logic actually helps

The scoring system in Colordle is brutal on saturation. If you are off on saturation, the score tanks harder than if you are slightly off on hue. With Sangria, if you guessed a bright fuchsia, your hue might have been technically close, but your saturation score destroyed you. Use that. If you get a low score on a bright guess, immediately dial down the intensity. Do not just shift the hue wheel.

Conversely, the logic rewards structural understanding. If you guess a color with high blue content and the game tells you the blue channel is way off, you know to pull back. But usually, the aggregate score tells the real story. A guess like #800000 (Maroon) might get you a decent hue match but fail on the specific pinkness. The gap between Maroon and Sangria is the green and blue balance. Maroon has almost no blue. Sangria has a healthy dose. The score difference between a Maroon guess and a Sangria guess highlights exactly how much purple is hiding in this red. Trust the gap. If two similar guesses yield vastly different scores, the difference in their hex codes is your roadmap.

Questions players keep asking

Is Sangria considered pink or red?

It is a red-dominant color, technically falling under the red hue family, but colloquially, most people would call this a dark pink or a wine color. In the context of the game, treat it as red with a heavy purple bias. If you are categorizing your mental palette, file it under "Reds" but mark it with a star for "high blue content." Do not file it under pinks, or you will miss the weight of the color. It is too dark to be a standard pink, but too vibrant to be a brown.

Why did my crimson guess fail so hard?

Crimson (#DC143C) is significantly more saturated and has less blue than Sangria. Crimson is a "loud" color. Sangria is a "quiet" color. The failure likely came from the saturation mismatch. The game penalizes high-saturation guesses when the target is muted. You were probably close on the red channel, but your green and blue channels were too low, making the color pop when it needed to recede. Next time, if a standard named red fails, assume the target is either much darker or much grayer.

How does this compare to previous wine colors?

We have seen variants like Merlot or Burgundy in the past. Those tend to be darker and browner (lower blue, higher red/green ratio). Sangria is distinct because it retains a certain pinkish vibrancy that pure burgundies lose. It is lighter than a typical Merlot. If you have a log of past answers, look for the ones where the blue channel was roughly 60% of the red channel. That ratio is the fingerprint of this specific wine shade. It is less about the name and more about the math of the hex code.

Should I guess named colors or hex values?

Always start with named colors to triangulate the region. Named colors like "Rose," "Maroon," and "Purple" act as excellent anchors. Once you know you are between Maroon and Purple, stop guessing names. Names are too sparse in this specific region of the color space. There are not enough named "wine" colors to cover the nuance. Switch to hex manipulation. Take your best named guess, note the error, and manually adjust the hex values. Increase the blue, decrease the red, add some green. Manual hex tuning is the only way to nail the exact desaturation level of Sangria.

2026-05-10.title: Colordle 1507: Lavender Is Not Just Light Purple
2026-05-10.summary: Today's Colordle answer is Lavender #B56EDC. If you burned early guesses on pastels, you probably overshot the brightness. This one sits in a weird middle ground that trips up the score logic.
bonusHints: Lavender has more blue in it than most people expect. If your red slider isn't cooperating, that's why.
Yesterday was Mocha, a warm brown. Today is a cool purple. The algorithm loves whiplash.
The 'V' in HSV for #B56EDC is 86. Not pastel, not deep. Right in the annoying zone.
If your guess was too gray, bump saturation before touching hue. Desaturated purples look like lavender but score terribly.
2026-05-10.articleHtml:
Today's Colordle answer at a glance

May 10, 2026 — Day 1507. The answer is Lavender, #B56EDC. If that hex code surprises you, you're not alone. Most people think lavender and picture something closer to a baby blanket — lighter, softer, barely-there purple. This isn't that. #B56EDC has real weight to it. It's a mid-tone purple with enough red to keep it warm and enough blue to stop it from veering into magenta territory.

After yesterday's Mocha (#9D7651), a dusty brown, the jump back into a saturated purple feels almost aggressive. Two days ago we had Mustard. Before that, Lemon and Ice. The game has been bouncing between muted earth tones and loud primaries all week. Lavender lands somewhere in between, which is exactly why it's tricky.

What kind of color this is

Lavender sits in the purple hue family, but calling it "just purple" misses the point. In HSL terms, it's got a hue around 280 degrees, saturation at roughly 60%, and lightness around 65%. That puts it firmly in the "medium-light, moderately saturated" bucket — the kind of color that doesn't announce itself loudly but also isn't washed out.

Compare it to something like Lilac or Wisteria, and Lavender pulls more toward blue. Compare it to a true violet, and it's noticeably softer. The hex code tells the real story: B5 red, 6E green, DC blue. That green channel is low, which is what keeps it from becoming a muddy brownish-purple. The blue channel is dominant, which is what gives it that cool, almost floral feel. If you've ever looked at actual lavender flowers up close, the dried buds are more muted, but the fresh blooms in direct sun hit something very close to #B56EDC.

One thing that catches players off guard: this color reads as "pastel" in memory but isn't one technically. Pastels usually have lightness above 80% and saturation below 30%. Lavender #B56EDC has too much pigment for that club. It's more accurate to call it a soft, saturated mid-tone.

How to narrow your guesses toward it

If you're starting from scratch, your first guess should aim to bracket the color space. A strong opening is something like a pure red, a pure blue, and a neutral gray. That trio tells you immediately where the hue lives and how much saturation you're dealing with. For today's answer, the blue guess would score well on hue proximity, the red guess would show you're in purple territory (since red + blue = purple), and the gray would reveal that this isn't a desaturated color.

Once you know you're in the purple range, the real work is dialing in lightness and saturation. The mistake pattern I see constantly: players land on the right hue family, then guess a very light lavender like #E6E6FA and get confused when the distance score is still high. That's because lightness mismatch hurts more than you'd think. Jump to a mid-tone purple next — something around #9966CC or #8A2BE2 — and watch how the scores tighten up.

From there, it's about fine-tuning the red-blue balance. Too much red and you're in orchid territory. Too much blue and you slide into periwinkle. Lavender wants roughly twice as much blue as red in the hex channels. If your guess has red and blue nearly equal, shift blue up by 20-30 points and red down by 10-15. You'll land in the right neighborhood fast.

Where players usually go wrong

The biggest trap with Lavender specifically is anchoring on the name. "Lavender" as a color name gets applied to everything from barely-tinted grays to vibrant purples in paint swatches and CSS named colors. The CSS color lavender is #E6E6FA, which is way lighter and less saturated than today's answer. If you know that hex code, it actively hurts you here. Today's Lavender is closer to what a florist would call lavender than what a web developer would.

Another common fail: players nail the hue and saturation but keep guessing colors that are too dark. Deep purples like eggplant or plum will get you a decent hue match score but tank your lightness score. The game's distance calculation punishes lightness errors harshly once you're inside the right hue band. I've watched people spiral through six guesses all within 20 degrees of the right hue, wondering why they're not closing the gap. It's almost always lightness.

The third pitfall is overcorrecting toward pink. When players see "purple with some red in it," they often swing too far and end up at colors like Thistle or Orchid. Lavender has red undertones, but they're subtle. If your guess looks like it belongs in a Valentine's Day palette, you've gone too far. Pull back toward blue.

How the score logic actually helps

Colordle's scoring isn't just a random distance number. It's calculating the Euclidean distance between your guess and the target in a three-dimensional color space — usually LAB or a similar perceptually uniform model. That means two guesses with the same RGB distance can score differently if one is perceptually closer to the human eye. The score logic is actually giving you better feedback than raw hex math would.

Here's how to read it practically: if your score is under 10, you're in the ballpark and should make tiny adjustments — single-digit hex tweaks. If your score is between 10 and 30, you've probably got the hue right but need to fix saturation or lightness. Above 30, and you're likely in the wrong hue family entirely or way off on brightness. Above 50, start over mentally. Your current guess is giving you almost no useful gradient to follow.

One specific trick for purple-range colors: pay attention to whether the game tells you your guess is "too red" or "too blue." That directional feedback is gold. If you're getting "too blue," warm it up with more red but keep the blue channel dominant. If you're getting "too red," don't just drop red — also bump green slightly to desaturate and shift the hue. Small, deliberate moves beat wild swings every time.

Questions players keep asking

Why isn't Lavender lighter? I thought lavender was a pastel.

Common misconception. In physical paint and dye, lavender is often a mid-tone. The pastel association comes from digital color palettes and CSS named colors, which use a very washed-out version. Today's #B56EDC is closer to real lavender flowers than the web-safe "lavender" you might know. The game pulls from a broad color database, not just web naming conventions.

How do I stop guessing purple variants that all look the same?

Stop using your eyes alone. After your second or third purple guess, switch to systematic adjustments. Change one hex channel at a time by increments of 20-30 and watch how the score responds. Your eyes will lie to you — two purples that look identical on screen can be 15 points apart in perceptual distance. Trust the numbers.

Does the game ever repeat colors?

Yes, eventually. With thousands of named colors in the pool, repeats are rare but they happen. We've seen Lavender appear before, though not recently. The game doesn't avoid repeats — it just has a big enough library that you won't see the same color often. If you play daily for a year, you'll probably hit a repeat or two.

What's the best starting guess for purple-heavy weeks?

If you suspect purple based on recent trends, don't guess purple first. Guess a strong blue and a strong red as your first two moves. The score feedback from those two guesses will triangulate the purple range better than any single purple guess would. Then use a neutral mid-gray to lock in saturation and lightness. Three guesses, and you've mapped the entire relevant color space.

2026-05-09.title: Colordle #1506: Mocha (#9D7651) - May 9, 2026
2026-05-09.summary: Today's answer is Mocha, a warm, mid-tone brown with strong red undertones. After yesterday's sharp Mustard, the game pivots to something earthier and more muted. If you started with cool primaries, you likely saw red hints but struggled with saturation. This guide breaks down the hex code #9D7651 and how to lock in that specific brownish-red balance without wasting guesses.
bonusHints: The hue family is red, but don't expect a vibrant crimson; think desaturated and earthy.
Saturation is moderate—this isn't a muddy gray, but it isn't neon either.
Lightness sits right in the middle, avoiding both deep chocolate and pale beige extremes.
If your red guess felt too bright or pink, dull it down and push it toward orange-brown.
2026-05-09.articleHtml:
Today's Colordle answer at a glance

The answer for May 9, 2026, is Mocha. The hex code is #9D7651. It is a mid-tone brown with a distinct red bias, sitting firmly in the red hue family but carrying enough yellow to feel warm rather than cool or purplish. After the jarring jump from Ice to Royal Blue earlier in the week, and yesterday's sharp Mustard, today's puzzle feels like a return to earth tones. It is not a tricky color in terms of obscurity, but it is easy to misjudge the exact ratio of red to brown.

Players who opened with standard primary reds likely got the hue direction correct but missed on saturation and lightness. Mocha is not a pure red; it is a desaturated, brownish variant. If your first guess was a bright fire-engine red, the game told you the hue was close but the intensity was way off. The challenge here is distinguishing between a dark orange, a true brown, and a reddish-brown. Mocha leans heavily into that reddish-brown space without crossing into terracotta or rust.

What kind of color this is

Mocha (#9D7651) is what happens when you take a solid red base and mute it significantly with gray and yellow. In the RGB model, the red channel (9D) is dominant, but the green (76) and blue (51) channels are high enough to kill the vibrancy. This creates that characteristic "coffee with milk" appearance. It is not a muddy color; it has warmth. Many browns can drift too green (olive) or too blue (chocolate), but Mocha stays warm because the red component is nearly double the blue component.

Visually, this color sits in a comfortable middle ground. It is not dark enough to be a shadow color, nor light enough to be a skin tone highlight. It is a structural color, often found in leather, wood, and yes, coffee. In the context of Colordle, it represents a classic "trap" color: players see "red family" and immediately saturate their next guess, pushing toward maroon or burgundy. Mocha rejects that. It demands you lower the saturation and accept a duller, earthier result. It is a humble color, lacking the flash of the Royal Blue from two days ago, but it is distinct once you isolate the warmth.

How to narrow your guesses toward it

Start by acknowledging the red hint. If your initial red guess screamed "too bright," do not switch to orange or purple yet. Instead, keep the hue angle near red but drastically reduce the saturation slider. You are looking for a grayish-red. Once you have a dull red, shift the lightness. Mocha is not dark; it is a medium tone. A common mistake is assuming all browns are dark. If you go too dark, you hit chocolate; too light, and you get beige. The sweet spot for #9D7651 is right around 50-60% lightness.

Another angle is to use the recent history. We had Mustard yesterday, which is high-saturation yellow-orange. If you take that Mustard energy and crush the saturation while shifting the hue back toward red, you land near Mocha. Think of it as "dirtier" and "redder" than Mustard. If you are using a color picker tool, look for hex codes where the Red value is roughly 160 (out of 255), Green is around 120, and Blue is near 80. The gap between Red and Green is key; if they are too close, you get gray. If Red is too high compared to Green, you get pink. Mocha needs that specific gap to maintain its brown identity.

Where players usually go wrong

The biggest pitfall today is over-correcting toward orange. Because Mocha is a brown, and brown is often associated with orange, many players will see a red hint and immediately swing to a pumpkin orange. This is incorrect. Mocha is fundamentally a red-hue color, not an orange-hue color. The difference is subtle but critical in the scoring logic. An orange guess will show a hue mismatch that sends people spiraling into yellow territory, wasting valuable attempts.

Secondly, players struggle with the "muddy" factor. When a color is desaturated, it is hard to tell if it is gray, brown, or just a dull version of a primary. Players often guess a pure gray to test saturation, then a pure brown. The issue is that "pure brown" isn't a standard color wheel position. You have to construct it. Players who guess distinct colors like "Rust" or "Terracotta" might get close, but those colors often lean too orange or too pink. Mocha is neutral-warm. It doesn't scream for attention. Treating it like a vibrant color is the fastest way to burn through your guess count.

How the score logic actually helps

Colordle's scoring system separates hue, saturation, and lightness, which is the only way to solve a color like Mocha efficiently. If the game only gave a single "distance" score, you would never know if you were too bright, too dark, or just the wrong shade of red. The split metrics allow you to isolate variables. If your hue score is green (close) but your saturation score is red (far), you know exactly what to adjust: keep the angle, drop the intensity.

For Mocha, the saturation metric is your best friend. A bright red guess will have a terrible saturation score. A dark chocolate guess will have a terrible lightness score. By observing which metric improves as you tweak your sliders, you can triangulate the position of #9D7651. For instance, if you move from a bright red to a duller red and the saturation score jumps from 20 to 80, you are on the right track. If you then darken it and the lightness score improves, you are converging. The logic doesn't lie; it just requires you to adjust one variable at a time rather than guessing randomly.

Questions players keep asking

Is Mocha considered a red or an orange?

In the strict geometry of the color wheel used by Colordle, Mocha is classified as red. While our eyes might perceive it as a brownish-orange in certain lighting, the underlying hex code #9D7651 derives its primary identity from the red channel. The green channel is present to dull it down, but the hue angle places it in the red sector, not the orange sector. Trust the red hint even if it looks brownish to you.

Why does my brown guess feel close but score poorly?

"Brown" is a range, not a point. You might have guessed a brown that leans green (olive drab) or blue (dark chocolate). Mocha is a warm, red-leaning brown. If your brown guess has too much blue in it, the hue distance will be significant even if the lightness and saturation look perfect. You need a brown that is essentially a dark, dull red.

How does this compare to yesterday's Mustard?

They are almost opposites in terms of saturation and hue stability. Mustard (#CEB301) is high saturation and sits on the border of yellow and orange. Mocha is medium-low saturation and sits firmly in red. If you tried to shift your Mustard guess toward red today without adjusting saturation, you likely ended up with a weird reddish-yellow. You have to kill the brightness to get from Mustard to Mocha.

Can I solve this without a color picker?

It is harder, but possible if you understand hex codes. Look at the first two digits (9D). That is high red. The middle two (76) are medium green. The last two (51) are low-medium blue. The fact that Red > Green > Blue is the signature of a warm brown. If your guess has Blue higher than Green, it is too cool. If Green is higher than Red, it is too yellow/green. Keep Red on top and the gaps proportional.

2026-05-08.title: Colordle 1505 Answer: Mustard (#CEB301) - May 8, 2026
2026-05-08.summary: Today's Colordle answer is Mustard (#CEB301), a deep, earthy orange that follows yesterday's bright Lemon. We break down why this hue family trips people up and how to use the score logic to lock in the win.
bonusHints: The answer sits firmly in the orange hue family but leans heavily toward yellow.
Expect high saturation; this isn't a pastel or muted tone.
Yesterday's Lemon was a strong temperature clue for today's solution.
The hex code starts with C, indicating a high red component mixed with green.
2026-05-08.articleHtml:
Today's Colordle answer at a glance

Mustard (#CEB301) is the target for May 8, 2026. If you spent all your guesses chasing cool tones or pure yellows, you aren't alone. This color sits in that tricky pocket where orange and yellow blur, often catching players off guard who expect distinct category separations. It is Day 1505, and the game decided to serve up something with serious weight after the brightness of Lemon yesterday.

The hex code #CEB301 tells the real story here. That 'CE' red value combined with 'B3' green creates a dense, warm tone that feels more food dye than sunset. It is not the neon pop of a construction cone; it is the stuff you put on a hot dog. Players who locked onto the 'yellow' label of yesterday's answer sometimes overshoot into pure gold today, missing the earthy undertone that defines Mustard.

What kind of color this is

Mustard lives in the orange hue family, but it is an orange that has seen some things. Unlike the vibrant, high-energy oranges we saw earlier in the week, this one feels grounded. It lacks the pinkish-red punch of a tangerine and avoids the pale wash of a cream. The saturation is high, meaning it is intense, but the brightness is moderated just enough to give it that characteristic tangy look.

Visually, it sits right between the electric yellow of Lemon (#FFF700) and a true amber. The lack of blue in the hex code (only '01') confirms there is zero coolness here. This is pure warmth. In design terms, this is an accent color that demands attention without screaming. It is the kind of color that looks great in small doses but overwhelms a room if you use too much. For Colordle purposes, treat it as a darkened, shifted yellow-orange.

How to narrow your guesses toward it

Start by anchoring your temperature. If your first guess was anywhere near blue, green, or purple, the game likely told you your temperature was cold. That is your cue to swing hard toward the warm side of the spectrum. Do not aim for red immediately, though. Red is too far. You need the bridge between yellow and red.

Try a pure orange first. If the game says you are close but the brightness is off, adjust the yellow channel. Mustard is darker than pure yellow but lighter than brown. If you guess a bright yellow like #FFFF00 and get a brightness indicator saying 'lower,' you are on the right track. Conversely, if you guess a brown and get 'higher,' you know you need to lift the luminance. The key is iterative adjustment of the green channel, which controls that yellow-orange shift.

Look at the recent history. We had Royal Blue and Plum earlier in the week, which are deep and cool. Then Ice, which is pale and cool. Yesterday was Lemon. The pattern suggests a move toward warm, saturated tones. Use that momentum. If you are stuck between two similar oranges, check the red value. Mustard has significant red, more than a lemon, less than a fire engine.

Where players usually go wrong

The most common mistake today is confusing saturation with brightness. Players see the word 'Mustard' or the visual cue of yellow-orange and immediately pick the brightest, most neon version they can find. They pick #FFD700 or similar golds. While close in hue, these often fail on the brightness metric. Mustard is denser. It has a viscosity to it.

Another trap is the 'yellow bias.' Because yesterday was Lemon, many players refuse to let go of the yellow category. They stay in the #FFFXXX range. This color requires you to accept that you are now in orange territory. The hue shift is significant enough that staying in pure yellow lands you far from the target. The game punishes hesitation to switch hue families even when the visual difference seems subtle.

Also, avoid the brown trap. Some players see the darker tone and panic, thinking it is a shade of brown or olive. They drop the saturation too low. Mustard is still very much a color, not a shade. Keep the saturation high. If your guess looks muddy, you have gone too far. The answer should still pop off the screen, just in a heavier way than Lemon did.

How the score logic actually helps

Colordle's scoring system is brutal but fair when you understand what it values. It does not just care if you are 'close'; it cares about the vector of your approach. When you guess a color like Mustard, the game evaluates the distance in 3D space. A guess that is slightly off in hue but perfect in brightness might score better than a guess with the right hue but wrong intensity.

Use the feedback to triangulate. If the game says your hue is correct but your brightness is wrong, do not change the hue. Many players tweak everything at once, which makes it impossible to know which variable caused the improvement. Change one axis at a time. Fix the brightness first, then fine-tune the hue. The score logic rewards precision over luck. A guess that is mathematically closer in RGB space gets the credit, even if it looks visually similar to a wrong guess.

Remember that the game calculates distance based on the cube root of the sum of squared differences. This means large errors in one channel (like guessing pure white when the answer is Mustard) hurt you more than small errors in all three. Focus on eliminating the biggest gaps first. If you are way off on red, fix that before worrying about the single digit in blue.

Questions players keep asking

Why did my orange guess feel right but score low?

This usually happens when the specific shade of orange is off. 'Orange' covers a massive range. You might have guessed a reddish-orange when Mustard is a yellow-orange. Or, more likely, your brightness was off. Mustard is specific. It is not a generic orange. Check if your guess was too light; if so, darken it while maintaining saturation. The hue family is broad, but the target is precise.

Is Mustard considered yellow or orange?

In the context of Colordle's hue families, it is classified as orange. However, it sits right on the boundary. This ambiguity is intentional. The game wants to see if you can distinguish between the primary yellow of Lemon and the secondary orange of Mustard. Treat it as orange for your hue guesses, but acknowledge the heavy yellow influence in your brightness adjustments.

How much does the previous day's answer matter?

It matters as a temperature anchor. Lemon (#FFF700) is extremely bright and pure yellow. Mustard (#CEB301) is the logical next step if the game wants to explore the yellow-orange spectrum. It lowers the brightness and adds red. If you ignored yesterday's Lemon and guessed a cool color today, you missed the contextual clue. The game often groups similar hues or complementary contrasts. Here, it is a gradient step.

What is the best first guess for colors like this?

A mid-tone orange or a saturated yellow-orange is ideal. Something like #FFA500 gives you a great baseline. It tells you immediately if you need more red, more yellow, or less brightness. Avoid starting with extremes like pure black, white, or neon green unless you are specifically testing the bounds of the color space. A balanced, saturated warm color yields the most data on day one.

2026-05-05.title: Colordle #1502 Answer: Royal Blue
2026-05-05.summary: Today's Colordle answer is Royal Blue (#4169E1), a vivid standard that sits right in the middle of the blue spectrum. After yesterday's muddy Plum and the day before's gray Slate, this primary punch feels intentional. We break down the hex math, why your green guesses failed, and how to lock in the score without burning extra attempts.
bonusHints: The red channel is significantly higher than you might expect for a 'blue' color.
This is a web standard color, not a CSS named variant like 'aliceblue'.
The green value is lower than the red, pushing it away from cyan.
Think of the classic flag blue, not the sky or navy.
2026-05-05.articleHtml:
Today's Colordle answer at a glance

Royal Blue (#4169E1) is the answer for May 5, 2026. If you spent your first few guesses hovering around teal or navy, you aren't alone. The leap from yesterday's Plum (#66386A) to this electric standard is jarring. We went from a desaturated, reddish-purple straight into a high-saturation primary. It feels like the game is resetting the board after a week of muddy mids and that brutal Vantablack start to the month.

This isn't a trick color. It's one of the original X11 web colors, defined back when monitors could barely handle 256 colors. It sits at a specific coordinate in RGB space that makes it brighter than Navy but darker than pure Blue (#0000FF). If you guessed a generic blue and got a score suggesting you were close on hue but off on brightness, Royal Blue is the correction. It's the exact shade you see on a lot of corporate logos and sports team jerseys because it pops on screens without blowing out the highlights.

What kind of color this is

Royal Blue lives in the blue hue family, but calling it just "blue" does a disservice to its specific character. Look at the hex: #4169E1. The red component is 65 (41 in hex), the green is 105 (69 in hex), and the blue is 225 (E1 in hex). That red value is the kicker. A pure blue has zero red. A cyan-leaning blue has high green. Royal Blue keeps the green moderate and bumps the red just enough to warm it up slightly, keeping it from feeling icy.

Compared to recent days, this is a saturation bomb. Slate (#516572) was practically gray. Plum (#66386A) was dark and muddy. Royal Blue is unapologetically vibrant. It's not a pastel, it's not a shade (black added), and it's not a tone (gray added). It's a pure hue with just enough adjustment to make it usable for text and UI elements without vibrating against white backgrounds. If your mental model of blue leans toward the cool, aquatic side (like our Mint guess from a few days ago), this will feel warmer than expected. It's a "dry" blue, like denim or ink, rather than water or sky.

How to narrow your guesses toward it

Start with the basics, but don't stop at "Blue." If you input #0000FF and the game tells you the score is close but the color feels too intense or cold, you need to add red. That's the secret sauce here. Many players try to fix a "too cold" blue by adding green, which pushes you toward cyan or teal. Royal Blue demands red. When you increase the red channel from 0 to around 65, you neutralize that harsh electric feel.

Watch the brightness carefully. If your guess is landing with a score that says "too dark," don't just crank up the lightness slider in your head. Check your blue channel. In Royal Blue, the blue channel is 225, which is high, but not maxed out. If you are guessing something like #2040A0, you are in the right hue family but the wrong weight. You need to lift that B value significantly. Conversely, if you guessed something like #6080FF, you're probably too light and too purple. The sweet spot is that 65-105-225 balance. It's heavier on the blue, obviously, but that 65 red is what keeps it from sliding into pure spectrum blue.

Where players usually go wrong

The biggest trap today is over-correcting for the previous days. After Plum and Slate, there's a temptation to guess "safe," desaturated colors. Players are guessing grays and browns, expecting the game to continue the dreary trend. Then they see the target is Royal Blue and panic. They swing the other way and guess Neon Blue or Electric Blue (#7DF9FF), thinking "vibrant" means "maximum brightness." That misses the mark. Royal Blue is vibrant, but it has weight to it. It's not a glowing light; it's a pigment.

Another common error is confusing Royal Blue with Navy. Navy (#000080) cuts the blue channel in half and kills the red and green. If your guess is coming back as "same hue, much darker," you're likely in Navy territory. Royal Blue needs that upper-mid range brightness. Also, avoid the purple trap. Because the red channel is non-zero, some players push it too far and guess Indigo or Violet. Remember, the green channel (105) is higher than the red (65). As long as Green > Red, you stay firmly in the blue zone. If Red > Green, you drift into purple. Keep your green value comfortably above your red value.

How the score logic actually helps

The scoring algorithm in Colordle weighs Euclidean distance in RGB space, but it feels the hue shift more than the brightness shift. If you guessed a color like #4060D0, your score should be incredibly high, even if the brightness is slightly off. The game penalizes hue errors (wrong mix of R/G/B ratios) much harder than luminance errors (overall lightness). This is why guessing pure primaries first is a solid strategy. If you guess pure Blue and the score is mediocre, you know immediately you need to adjust the R/G ratio, not just the brightness.

Use the score to triangulate the red component. If you guess a standard blue and the feedback suggests you're warm (metaphorically speaking), check your red channel. In today's case, a lot of standard blues have zero red. The fact that Royal Blue has 65 units of red is a significant deviation from the pure spectrum. If your first guess was #0000FF and the distance metric was large, your next guess should introduce red immediately. Try #4040FF. If the score improves, you know you need more red. If it gets worse, you've overshot into purple. The math doesn't lie: the distance between #0000FF and #4169E1 is substantial because of that red injection. Don't ignore the red channel just because the name says "Blue."

Questions players keep asking

Why did my pure blue guess score so low?

Pure blue (#0000FF) lacks the red component that defines Royal Blue. The distance in RGB space between 0 red and 65 red is significant enough to tank your score. The game sees them as distinct neighbors, not twins. You need to add that red warmth to get the high score.

Is Royal Blue considered a warm or cool color?

It's a cool color, but it's a "warm" blue. Compared to a cyan or ice blue, it has heat. Compared to red or orange, it's freezing. In the context of today's puzzle, treat it as a cool color that refuses to be icy. It has enough red to keep it grounded, unlike the sterile feel of computer-default blue.

How is this different from the blue on the Facebook logo?

Facebook's blue has shifted over the years, but it often leans closer to #1877F2 or similar variants. Royal Blue (#4169E1) is slightly more purple and slightly darker than the typical "tech blue" which often tries to maximize brightness for LCD screens. Royal Blue feels more traditional, like a crayon or a paint tube, whereas tech blues often feel engineered for emissive displays.

Can I use a color picker to solve this faster?

You can, but you risk bypassing the logic puzzle. If you just sample a royal blue swatch, you get the answer without understanding the RGB relationship. The better move is to use the picker to test your hypothesis. Guess a color, then pick a swatch that looks similar to the target. Compare the hex codes. See that your guess had 0 red and the target has 65. That delta is the lesson. Use the tools to learn the math, not just to fill the box.

2026-05-04.title: Colordle 1501 Answer: Plum (#66386A) - May 4, 2026
2026-05-04.summary: Today's Colordle answer is Plum, a deep, muted purple with hex code #66386A. After yesterday's cool Slate, we're diving back into rich tones that sit right on the edge of neutral. If you burned guesses on bright magentas or leaned too hard into blue-violets, this one stung. Here is how to lock down this specific shade next time.
bonusHints: Think less electric purple and more dried flower.
The red channel is lower than you might expect for a 'purple'.
It sits darker than standard web purple but lacks the blackness of Vantablack.
Check the balance between blue and red; they are nearly even.
2026-05-04.articleHtml:
Today's Colordle answer at a glance

We are looking at Plum for May 4, 2026. The hex code is #66386A. This is day 1501, and frankly, it is a tricky one if you came in expecting something vibrant. We just had Vantablack two days ago and Slate yesterday, so the jump to a color with actual chroma felt jarring for some. But Plum isn't your standard-issue purple. It is dusty, muted, and sits heavily in the mid-tones.

If you guessed bright orchid or neon violet, the game told you immediately that you were way off on saturation. This color lives in the shadows. It is the kind of shade you find in old upholstery or overripe fruit left on the counter. The specific hex values tell the real story: Red is 102, Green is 56, and Blue is 106. Notice how close the red and blue values are? That near-equality is what kills the brightness and gives it that distinctively flat, plum-like appearance.

What kind of color this is

Plum (#66386A) is a desaturated dark violet. Calling it just "purple" feels lazy because it lacks the punch of a primary purple. It leans slightly more blue than red, but only by a hair. In the HSL spectrum, this thing is sitting around 298 degrees hue, which is standard purple territory, but the saturation is where it gets interesting. It is barely hanging on to about 30% saturation. That is why it feels so heavy.

Compare this to the Vermilion from April 30th. Vermilion screams for attention with high saturation and brightness. Plum whispers. It absorbs light rather than reflecting it. The lightness value hovers around 24%, making it significantly darker than a medium purple but not quite black. It occupies that uncomfortable middle ground where colors often lose their identity and just look "muddy" if you aren't careful with your guesses. It is a sophisticated, if somewhat depressing, color.

How to narrow your guesses toward it

Start by establishing the floor. Since we had Vantablack recently, your brain might be wired to think "dark" means "no color." Do not make that mistake. Plum has color, just very little light. Your first guess should test the water temperature between blue and red. Try a standard medium purple like #800080. If the game tells you the hue is close but the brightness is wrong, do not swing wildly to pink or indigo.

Instead, drag your slider down into the grays. The key to finding #66386A is reducing saturation before you adjust the hue. Most players overshoot on the red channel because they associate "plum" with redder fruits. But this specific hex code is cool-toned. Once you lock in the purple-ish hue, stop touching the hue slider. Focus entirely on the brightness and saturation knobs. You need to dull it down. Add gray. Make it muddy. If your guess looks like a crayon, you are still too bright. Keep desaturating until it looks like a shadow.

Where players usually go wrong

The biggest trap here is the name association. People hear "Plum" and think of the skin of a ripe Santa Rosa plum, which can be surprisingly bright and pinkish-red. Or they think of plum juice, which is deep but vibrant. The web color "Plum" is actually a light lavender, which is the exact opposite of today's answer. If you aimed for any version of light plum, you wasted a guess.

Another common error is ignoring the green channel. In a color this dark, the green value (56) is significant. It is not zero. If you try to force a pure blue-red mix with no green, you end up with a digital purple that looks synthetic. Real-world dark purples almost always have enough green in them to kill the vibrancy. Players who kept their green channel at zero or near-zero kept getting feedback that their color was too "clean" or "electric." You need that dirt in the mix.

How the score logic actually helps

Colordle's distance metric is brutal on low-saturation colors because the perceptual difference between "close" and "way off" shrinks. When you are dealing with a color like #66386A, moving the slider by 5 points can feel like a massive jump in the score. Use that. If your score drops significantly when you lower the brightness by a tiny increment, you know you are in the right neighborhood.

Also, look at the hue shift. If you are at 290 degrees and the score suggests you are far, do not just spin the wheel to 300. Check if the issue is actually saturation. Often, a high distance score on a muted color means your saturation is too high, not that your hue is wrong. The algorithm penalizes high saturation errors heavily on dark colors because they stand out more against the target. Treat the score as a saturation gauge first, hue gauge second.

Questions players keep asking

Why does Plum look different on my screen?

Because #66386A is so close to gray, monitor calibration plays havoc with it. On a high-contrast OLED, this might look like a rich eggplant. On a washed-out laptop screen, it might just look like a dirty gray. Do not trust your eyes blindly today. Trust the numbers. If the RGB values are roughly equal with blue slightly dominant, you are there, even if it looks brownish to you.

Is this considered a warm or cool color?

It is technically cool because of the blue dominance, but it is so desaturated that it acts neutral. It does not have enough heat to be warm, despite the red component. In a design context, you would use this as a neutral, similar to how you used Slate yesterday, not as an accent color.

How does this compare to yesterday's Slate?

Slate (#516572) was blue-green dominant and felt industrial. Plum (#66386A) is red-blue dominant and feels organic, albeit decaying organic matter. Both share that low-light, high-gravity feel. If you nailed Slate by keeping things muted, you should have been able to pivot to Plum by just shifting the hue toward red and killing the green.

What is the best next guess if I missed today?

Try a color with the same lightness but higher saturation to see which direction the hue shifts. Or, pick a color like Indigo (#4B0082) to test the lower bound of the purple spectrum. You need to triangulate the muddiness. Do not guess a bright color next; stay in the dark, muted lane to refine your understanding of the game's current preference for heavy tones.

2026-04-29.title: Colordle Answer for April 29, 2026 - Cool Blue #4984B8
2026-04-29.summary: Day 1496 brings us Cool Blue, a steady blue with green undertones that sits comfortably in the mid-range saturation. If you've been tracking recent colors, you'll notice we've bounced from earth tones to primaries and back. This one rewards players who think in hue families rather than chasing specific shades.
bonusHints: The green undertone is subtle but real—don't lock yourself into pure blue guesses if you've already tried those.
Saturation-wise, you're looking at something neither muted nor electric. Mid-range territory.
Check yesterday's Spelunking—if you noted how far that dark blue-green was from pure blue, this one sits roughly opposite on the saturation scale.
Recent history shows a pattern: after the wild card that was Purple (#800080), we're back to something more predictable. Lean into that.
2026-04-29.articleHtml:
Today's Colordle answer at a glance

April 29, 2026. Day 1496. The answer is Cool Blue, hex code #4984B8. This one falls squarely in the blue hue family but carries enough green in its mix to keep players who guess purely cyan or navy guessing. The saturation sits at a comfortable mid-range—vibrant enough to register clearly, muted enough that it won't punch you in the face like a neon.

If you've been playing daily, you know the last few days have been a ride. Yesterday gave us Spelunking, that moody dark blue-green. Before that, the actual purple (#800080) showed up and threw off anyone who doesn't see purple as a primary color. Sunshine and Kiwi rounded out the week with warm tones. Now we're back to blue territory, but not the deep ocean kind. Think of a clear sky on a temperate day—not the intense summer blue, but the reliable blue that shows up most often.

The hex code breaks down as 49 red, 84 green, and B8 (184) blue. That green value being higher than red tells you something. It's not a blue with purple leanings. It's a blue that wants to be friendly, approachable, almost teal-adjacent without crossing over. Players who understand hex composition will spot the green undertone immediately. Those relying on pure color memory might need an extra guess or two.

What kind of color this is

Cool Blue is exactly what it sounds like—a blue that doesn't try too hard. It's the color of a default link on a well-designed website, the blue in corporate logos that want to seem trustworthy without being boring, the sky most stock photos use when they need something that reads as optimistic but professional.

In design terms, this sits in the "accessible blue" category. It has enough contrast against white and dark backgrounds to be readable without being aggressive. Web designers pick colors like this when they need something that won't alienate anyone. It's the blue of buttons that say "Continue" or "Submit." Not exciting, but effective.

The hue angle for #4984B8 puts it around 210 degrees on the color wheel, which is classic blue territory. What pushes it away from pure blue is the saturation and lightness. At roughly 50% saturation and 55% lightness, it's neither washed out nor deeply saturated. Think of it as the blue equivalent of a medium-voiced person in a meeting—present, clear, and easy to understand.

What makes this color interesting is its versatility. It works as a primary color in palettes, as an accent against warm backgrounds, or as a neutral-adjacent option when you need something less aggressive than royal blue but more defined than powder blue. Players who think in terms of use cases will recognize this as a "safe but competent" blue—the kind designers reach for when they need universal appeal.

How to narrow your guesses toward it

Start with any blue in the mid-saturation range. Colors like steel blue, cornflower, or your standard medium blue will all return useful information. The game will tell you how close you are through its scoring system, which we'll get into later, but the real narrowing happens when you test the green undertone.

If you guess a pure blue—something like #0000FF or a standard medium blue without green leanings—you'll see that the green component is off. That feedback tells you to head toward cyan or teal territory. But don't overshoot into full cyan (#00FFFF) because that swings the saturation too far. You're looking for something that sits between cyan and blue, closer to blue than cyan, but definitely not pure blue.

Another approach: test a few colors in the blue family with varying lightness. Start with a lighter blue to establish the lightness floor, then go darker to find the ceiling. Since this color sits at medium lightness, you'll find it sandwiched between pastel blues and navy blues. Once you've bracketed the lightness, you can fine-tune the hue.

The hex code structure gives you another angle. The red value (49) is significantly lower than green (84) and blue (184). If you guess something like #4477CC, you're in the right ballpark but might be slightly too saturated or too purple-leaning. Adjust from there based on feedback. Each guess should eliminate a dimension—whether that's hue, saturation, or lightness—so you're converging on #4984B8 within three to four educated guesses if you're reading the clues well.

Where players usually go wrong

The biggest mistake with this particular color is assuming "blue" means "pure blue." Players who immediately guess #0000FF or a standard medium blue without checking the hex composition often waste a guess getting the saturation wrong. They see blue, they guess blue, and then they're confused when the score doesn't match expectations.

Another common error: overcorrecting toward cyan. When players realize this blue has green in it, some swing too far and guess something like #00B8D4 or a full cyan. That sends them down the wrong branch of the color tree. The green undertone is subtle—present but not dominant. You want a blue that contains green, not a green that contains blue.

Players also struggle with the lightness. This isn't a light blue (think sky blue, powder blue) and it's not a dark blue (think navy, midnight). It's the blue of a business casual outfit—present but not loud. People who anchor on lighter blues as their mental reference for "soft blue" will keep guessing too light. Those who anchor on dark blues for "deep blue" will keep guessing too dark. The sweet spot is in the middle.

Finally, there's the recent colors trap. Seeing Purple (#800080) yesterday makes some players gun for colors with purple in them today. Don't. Each day's answer is independent. The purple was a one-off, a reminder that the game can throw curveballs. Today is back to straightforward territory. Cool Blue is the opposite of a trap—it's exactly what it claims to be.

How the score logic actually helps

The Colordle scoring system gives you three pieces of information for each guess: exact match, partial match, and miss. Understanding how to read these together is what separates quick solvers from those who burn through guesses without making progress.

For this color, an exact match means you've nailed the hue, saturation, and lightness for that particular channel. A partial match means you're in the right neighborhood but off on the specifics. The system doesn't just tell you "you're wrong"—it tells you how you're wrong, which is the actual advantage of playing a logic-based color game rather than a pure guessing game.

When you guess a color and see that the blue channel is exact but green is only partial, you know to preserve the blue value while adjusting green. If you guessed #4488CC and got exact on red, partial on green, and partial on blue, you'd know that red is locked (or very close), green needs movement in the right direction, and blue is in the ballpark but not perfect. That feedback should tell you to bump green up slightly and adjust blue based on whether you're too light or too saturated.

The scoring also helps you avoid wasted guesses on obviously wrong branches. If you've guessed three blues and none of them returned any exact matches, you might be in the wrong hue family entirely. But since we know this is blue, the logic should guide you toward the correct saturation and lightness rather than cycling through unrelated hues.

Think of it as a three-dimensional coordinate system. Each guess narrows your position in that space. The goal isn't to guess the exact color in one shot—that's nearly impossible. The goal is to converge on it through logical elimination, using the score feedback as your map. Players who treat each guess as a data point rather than a hope tend to solve in fewer attempts.

Questions players keep asking

Is Cool Blue the same as Steel Blue or Cornflower Blue?

Similar, but not identical. Steel Blue (#4682B4) runs slightly darker and more saturated. Cornflower Blue (#6495ED) is lighter and more saturated with a hint more purple. Cool Blue sits between them in lightness but closer to Steel Blue in saturation. If you guessed either of those, you'd be in the right hue family and reasonably close on saturation, but you'd likely need one more adjustment to nail the exact hex. The game would show you partial matches pointing toward the specific differences.

Why does this color feel harder than yesterday's Spelunking?

Spelunking (#35465E) was dark and desaturated—a narrow slice of color space that's relatively distinctive. Cool Blue sits in a crowded neighborhood. There are dozens of blues in the medium saturation, medium lightness range. The closer colors are to each other, the harder they are to distinguish, which means you need more precision in your guesses. With Spelunking, you could be off by a few hex values and still get useful feedback. With Cool Blue, you need to be more precise because nearby blues will return similar scores until you hit the right one.

Should I check the recent colors before guessing?

It helps, but not in the way you might think. Recent colors don't influence today's answer—the game generates each day's color independently. However, looking at recent colors helps you calibrate your monitor and your color perception. If you've been playing on a screen that runs warm, you might perceive this blue as slightly green when it's actually neutral. Seeing the exact hex values of recent colors and comparing them to what you saw gives you a baseline for how your display handles blues versus yellows versus greens. Use history for calibration, not prediction.

What's the best first guess for this color?

Something in the mid-range blue family that you know well. A standard medium blue like #4169E1 (Royal Blue) or #4682B4 (Steel Blue) works well as a baseline. You want a guess that gives you clean data—not a color so far off that everything comes back as misses, but not so close that you get lucky on the first try without learning anything. A baseline guess establishes your starting position in color space. From there, the feedback tells you which direction to move. If Royal Blue returns partial matches on green and blue, you know to adjust those channels. If it returns mostly misses, you're further off than expected and need to make bigger jumps.

Does the hex code structure give any shortcuts?

Yes. The hex format breaks into RGB channels, and you can use that structure to make systematic adjustments. Red is at 49 (low), green at 84 (medium), blue at 184 (high). If you're guessing and see that your green is too high, you know to pull it back. If your blue is too low, push it up. Each channel is independent, so you can solve each one separately and combine them. Players who think in hex rather than color names tend to solve faster because they're working with numbers rather than subjective descriptions like "too purple" or "not saturated enough."
